section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT SCOUT SNIPER BASIC FIELD GUIDE
content:
- PART 7
Hello folks, this is ICT with the Scout Sniper Basic Field Guide part 7 in our continuing series. After this, we
only got one more installment left and we're complete. We're going to be looking at multiple targets and
what specifically will be covered in this presentation:
- Reviewing the previous assignment
- Discovering the average number of pips intraday study on that
- Looking at the ICT chart examples and stop-loss orders revisited
- Looking at the average daily pip ranges
- Discussing when ranges contract and why it's essential for you to be prepared
- The 30-pip stop loss and why
- Looking at the ICT split gains ratio
- Why is it important to understand your trade setups
- What is the concern, what makes a trade different to any other
- Why is classification essential to profit taking
- The ICT split ratios explained
- Discussing are we in sync or not
- Intermediate term ratios
- Short-term ratios
- Day-trading ratios
- Finally scalping ratios
In conclusion, we're going to have one more assignment. It's the last one in this series. It will be basically a
review for you to go through all of the previous videos one more time, try to gather up all your notes again,
and we'll be moving on to the final conclusion installment to this series. I think what I have planned and
outlined is still a little bit more work to be complete, but what I have so far, you'll be very surprised to see
how these tools are used and when they use them and when not to use them.

We're looking at the Euro/USD, a 15-minute chart, and keeping in mind with this teaching series, obviously
we talked about how trading in line with the higher time frame daily and using the 9 and 18 moving
averages expansion with the images rather, to kind of give you a buy model for institutional flows. We'll use
this section of the marketplace, basically the 30th of September through the 4th of October, so you can pull
that up on your charts. Everybody can do the same markups I'm doing here.
I have each day delineated with vertical lines, and you can do that in MT4 by holding down control and
tapping Y. These horizontal lines delineate the previous day. This is the previous day's high, which is seen
here, and this is the previous day's low, which is seen here. I'm going to share these tools on the internet,
and you'll be able to download and apply them to your MT4 platform as well.

We're going to look at a few things here, obviously beginning with the average pip range. This day's high to
low, using this here, we have 78 pips. So, when this particular week, this Monday, it was 70 pips for that
particular day. This trading day, we had the high here and the low right there on that candle. By looking at
that range, we have a range of 71 pips, very close to the previous day's daily range.
We have this range here, which is 103 or 104 pips rather. Again, that's this day's high, this day's low. What
this indicator does, it actually plots the previous day's high and low so you can see graphically. There's a lot
of uses for that, but we're not going to go into it here, but you'll hear more about it as we get into the eighth
installment in this series. We have 64 pips on this range here.

What did we just see? We saw 70, 70 is, you know, the pip range hanging around that 70 to 80 pip marker.
We move into 100 pips, then here we shrunk down to 60. So we had 70, 70s, 100, 60. So we had a
contraction in the daily range right in here. That means there is a pretty sizable move that's expected just
simply looking at volatility alone. This is why it's important to know when the daily range starts to contract.
You need to be prepared because something's about to take place that you could capitalize on. We're
going into a new week here, the market makes a small range again, and that's this low here, this high.
Typically, Mondays we don't like to trade, but you can see the high and low here on this day. It's this range
here, about 45, so the daily pip ranges have really consolidated into a smaller, tighter daily range. So we're
much, much smaller than we were on this previous week. Price comes down, finds some support. We have
the previous day's high here, which is right here, this candle here, and the low here. So we're going to put
on this so it's easy to see. 113 pips is this range here. So the ranges are starting to expand.

Previous day's range as well, we have 59, so we have another small contraction in daily range. So we
would expect another move of significance. Now, it doesn't give you directional premise. Before I go any
further, I don't want to confuse you here. When the ranges start to contract, that does not imply directional
premise. It just means a volatility pop, if you will, where the market is really going to make a swing one
direction or the other is highly probable. We're going to add some directional stuff in a moment.
We have this daily high here and daily low, which is seen on Sunday's candle here, our range, which we
don't like to do too much trading on Sunday. Here's Tuesday of that week's daily range. You can see that is
communicated here, and we start moving into more larger daily ranges, for example, above 90 here. And
prior to this range here, we had on that Monday of this particular week, we have a range of 53 pips. So we
started to shrink up and we exploded, we had a very nice increase in the daily range, 161 pips, which was
seen here.

What I'm trying to draw your attention to is not only do we look at price action on the charts, but we're also
measuring and keeping an eye on volatility. Because when the ranges start to get small, or if they drop off
over the last 5 days much lower than the average daily range, we're moving into an area where we're
probably going to have a very sharp price move. And again, that does not give you a prognostication
influence, I know, it doesn't give you any kind of directional premise if you will. It just gives you a neon sign
that everybody looks for that something's about to happen. Well, this is one of the best things you can have
because it's a shrinking of volatility. You have like this quiet before the storm.
Because we're looking at a 15-minute chart and looking at 'one shot one kill' per week, one cherry-picked
scenario for the whole entire week that we really try to hone in on for an opportunity with the highest
probability of profitability and the lowest probability of risk. That's what a professional trader spends his
career doing; that's his routine. He looks for those conditions. There's a lot of opportunities that he'll let go
or she'll let go by with no regard whatsoever about who else is getting it. You, in your development towards
a professional trader, you want to be having that mindset as well. You want to be moving towards a level of
consistency and not feeling like a dog in a meat market, just running around chasing every little thing
hanging off of a counter, nibbling here and nibbling there and never really being satisfied. I can't express
how satisfying it is to sit on your hands and wait for a specific environment, specific condition, specific
setup, and then watch it unfold exactly how you have waited for it patiently, and then take advantage of it.
Execute, no fear, no worry, no rushing, absolute patience and there's no emotion whatsoever. The emotion
comes on the other side of the trade when you get out, hopefully with profitability, hopefully with having
very little drawdown during the trade. So, moving in that direction as a developing trader, that's where your
mindset should be early on. And if you haven't done that and you've been trained for a while, you'd be
surprised if you started doing that very thing how much more significant your results would be, and it would
clear a lot of the emotional and fear and greed aspects that come inherent with trading. So, understanding
the contraction of the daily ranges again is vital to understanding when the next big move quote-unquote is
about to ensue, because we as traders, that's what we need, we need volatility to make money. If the
markets don't move, it's very difficult for us to make money, unless you're an options trader, which, you
know, I'm not going to be claiming to be an avid, you know, options trader, but there's certainly means of
taking advantage of the marketplace when there is quiet markets. I'm just not that type of trader. I need
action.

Let's go back and look at a few things. In the previous video, we talked about stop loss placement and
managing trailing stops and such, and I really want to hone this down to a very core principle so you can
understand what it is specifically that you're trying to do when you place your stop and why it's important to
have a specific number of pips as a, is a like a template, if you will.
Because we're trading with a 'one shot one kill' mentality, looking for one choice setup for the week. We
understand we looked at a few daily ranges here. While it is not always 100 pips, I classify the average
daily range for the Euro and maybe the British Pound USD, in other words, cable and fiber pairs, I classify
them as a generic 100-pip daily range. Now please do not send emails to me or message me on Twitter
saying Michael, the fiber doesn't have an average daily range of 100 pips every day or it hasn't had it for
this number of days or what. I know that it will move in and out. But in my mind, I adopt a 100-pip daily
range. Will it always have exactly 100 pips? No. Will it go over? It certainly will. Will it double it sometimes?
Certainly. Will it go very, very small and have even a 40-pip or less daily range? Yes, it can do that. So
please don't try to start an argument with me because I'm really not interested. I'm showing you how I view
the market; it's up to you whether you discern if it's any value or not. But because I have framed it with an
average of 100 pips on a daily basis, one third that is about 30 pips.

section_title: MARKET LOW FORMATION AND TRADING FLEXIBILITY
content:
My premise to trading with a 'one shot one kill' mentality is if we're in an environment where we already
expect bullish prices. If we expect bullish prices, we've looked at the higher time frame daily chart, maybe
the 9-day and 18-day are signifying that it is a buy model right now. Everything is institutional flows are
moving higher. We've come off of nice higher level support levels and breaking resistance levels, everything
is bullish.
We've already learned that the market finds its low for the week typically between Monday and
Wednesday's London open. Very high probability for Tuesday's London open to create it, but again, we're
flexible because we are not trading in black and white, we trade in the gray. So we have to thrive in the gray.
We have a general rule to expect the high or low to form by Wednesday's London open, but there's going
to be small deviations in that periodically. It's just simply because we want it to be like that always, it's not
always going to be like that, so you have to have some flexibility in trading, and that goes along with your
analysis as well.

We're going to go back over a few concepts with this whole week in mind, and again, we're looking at the
September 30th to October 4th. For time reduction on the video, again, I'll just counsel you to go to your
daily chart, pull up your moving averages just to confirm this is indeed a time frame when the market is
showing institutional flows being bullish, with the average is crossing and everything trying to move higher.
We have Wednesday's trading here. We have Tuesday's trading here, and Monday, we had a range from
Tuesday's high here and Monday's low. So, the market made a high here in an early week, took out this
high here and traded back and receded back into Wednesday. Now, this candle here, move my little things
here so I can see what I'm looking at, this candle comes in at 12:15 GMT or 12:30 GMT rather. Now, that's
classic New York open. So while we did have the market make a nice little low here during its London
session here, New York came down, swept that low out, and then the real move came. So we talked about
that stop rating event. But more specifically, we're going to go a little closer.

section_title: IDENTIFYING ORDER BLOCKS AND OPTIMAL ENTRY
content:
You can see what we have here, this is that Monday and Tuesday. We're going to pull up the range, calling
it too low, dropping our fib right on this high. So we have, we've outlined our range here. And we also know
that because the market has been moving lower, we're bullish, we're looking for buys in this environment
based on a daily timeframe, we were looking for bullish scenarios.
So we have our fib from the low here up to the high here. Go out just one more little click here, as you can
see, we're looking at here's that Monday low, Tuesday's high, and markets finding lower prices, and then
we move into this is London open for that Wednesday on the 2nd of October, and then the New York open
comes down, raids those lows. See how clean that is? They're just about the same level here, and that's a
real high suspect area where a pocket of stops will be resting or a liquidity pocket, and then our liquidity
pool rather. And the market comes down, raids that area here, assumes all those pending orders that
would be what it would be sell orders. There would be sell orders because anyone would be trying to buy
long in here, they're protecting in their mind by having a stop loss in the form of a sell stop because if the
prices come down lower, they want to be getting out the market. That sell stop becomes a market order to
do what? Sell at the market. Who would be willing to take the other side of the trade? Those that are in the
know. So we need to price down here and you saw that rally up. Here's what we want to do. We want to
know on the 2nd of October, when price was dropping down, was there something over here that it was
trying to fulfill?

Well, if you go out to an hourly chart, here is the bearish candle prior to the bullish move up. Let's zoom in
a little bit more. We have price coming down, touching that hourly order block prior that big move here on
the 30th. Then we move higher here on that Tuesday, and then we have the market receding down. Notice
that price comes right down into that sweet spot as well, but does not violate the 79 percent retracement
level. It comes right down to the order block here. Just take this off so you can clearly see what I'm referring
to, that's how precise it gets. So when price comes down, hits that level, it starts to rally up. Now here's
where we're going to introduce the concept of the effective stop loss placement.

- KILL ZONES AND KILLING GROUND
Let's go back down to the 15-minute time frame. We're going to show this is the whole London session
open, not the whole session, it's just the London open as defined by me. And here is the New York session,
as defined by me. We're going to assume again that all things that we discuss in this video series are
favoring an area to be buying in here.
We're going to highlight this area here. Again, we're looking at this point here, it's the beginning of time and
price in this area. Here is where we'd be looking, all that to the last portion of New York session. So what I
do is that this outside edge of this rectangle is right here, and the beginning of this rectangle edge is
approximately beginning here. So what I've done was I've mapped out this whole area of where time and
price are conducive for a buy. So now we're going to take the fib off. 1.3510 is a nice round number. We're
going to assume that this level here is what we traded. I'm not going to use this as the ideal scenario. So
I'm going to show you how powerful this stuff is and you don't need to be so precise. This whole area
you've identified as a level of opportunity. So this is a killing ground inside of kill zones; these are kill zones,
this is a killing ground. This is an area where we'd be stalking a setup. We don't look for any setups during
this time period; it's dead low in the marketplace. This is like quiet time. You want to be trading in New York
open, I'm sorry, London open rather, or the New York open. But we're looking for this move for the weekly
range to continue moving higher. So as the market moves from Tuesday's high down into Wednesday's
London open, this is an area where we would be hunting a setup.

section_title: APPLYING THE 30-PIP STOP LOSS IN PRACTICE
content:
If we used this price level here as 1.3507, let's assume for a moment we traded at 1.3515 right in here.
Nice little area, it's previous day's low in here, right in here. So we're going to use that as our entry point
because dealing ranges and bank traders like to use previous days highs and lows. We're going to assume
for a moment that we took that trade long in here in this consolidation little area in here. We went long
there. If we use, let's get rid of this rectangle here, if we use 30 pips as a stop, our stop would reside down
here, 1.3486 or thereabouts. And our entry would be approximately, let's go down to where we said, 1.3515.
That's showing 28 pips. I would need this to go a little bit deeper. And that's why again, I like to use 30 pips,
because let's look at this. With this range, from that low and that daily range, look how much of that daily
range that it consumes in terms of, you know, total daily range here. Look from this day's high, it's
Tuesday's high. Look how much of that daily range is consumed with that price move, that magnitude of
price. Again, the low forming right here on that candle right there, that's the daily low. Look how much of
that daily range has been consumed with 30 pips. So if you combine the mechanics of entering the
marketplace at a specific time during a specific kill zone like London or New York open, specifically looking
for the setup here, even with this stop raid right there, you are not going to miss that opportunity to get
long, you're not going to. So when you see this move, price stabbing down like this, by having all these
levels identified, you would at least be willing to see it trade down to that point here and your stop-loss isn't
triggered, you're still a good chunk away in terms of pips, there's no reason for you to be fearful of that. So
again, 30 pips.

section_title: STRATEGIC STOP LOSS AND MANAGING A TRADE
content:
30 pips. If you are timing your trade with 'one shot one kill', looking for the choice days to be looking for the
low to be forming Monday, Tuesday or Wednesday. Even with imperfection with your entry point, this is why
you use 30 pips. And for those of you who've already started thinking earlier on the video, how I picked the
most cherry-picking scenario there is, now I gave this scenario to show you how even when my tools did
not give me the perfect entry but still allow me to be involved in the marketplace. These lows in here resting
below that would be a nice area of stops. So it'd be a liquidity pool there.
The market rallies up a little bit and comes right back down, takes those participants out. This is the trick
that you just swing in here just so that false move lower. Everyone would be chasing that prior to move
higher. So we have the London open to New York open. By having that, that framework, we could be long in
here looking for a move going into Friday. Here's Thursday's trading that week, and Friday does not make a
higher high in here. But now let's go back to the stop-loss, why and how we manage them. I'm going to
show why again, I use this specific example because again, it shows you how you get two lines portion of
the move and you're not absolutely getting the low and you're not absolutely getting the high. So now let's
assume for a moment you've went long in here, your stop-loss obviously, as we just detailed, is down here.
So by having this entry point, the market moves up. We're looking at a 15-minute time frame. What you're
going to look for are the swing lows on the 15-minute timeframe, and you're going to be looking at the most
recent to as the market starts to move up. You took out the previous day's high here, right there. So it's this
swing low, and then when this swing low forms, here's this one and this one. So now you can move your
stop loss from 1.3485 up to this low here, just a little bit below it. So now by having that setup like that.

So now again, looking at how the market moves higher, this would be a nice level to take some profits. But
now we're going to be looking to preserve our open position. So if we took a portion of the trade off in this
area here based on the previous day's high, it's a nice reason to take it out, not all of the trade, but some of
it. We have when the market starts to move up and take out this high here, we have this low in this low, but
now we have this swing low here. So now you have this low and this low, so now you can move your stop
up to just below this low. So over here, your stop loss would be right about in this vicinity right here. The
market comes down, comes down, does not re-raid it.
As the market starts to make a lower low here than this one here, are you nervous? Probably are, but are
you stopped out? No. So what do you follow, the rules or your emotions? The rules. So now when the
market starts to rally up here and takes out this high here, right on this candle here, you look at where
we're at in terms of swing lows. We have this swing low and this swing low, which is the lowest of them, the
two most recent swing lows, it's this one here. So you would be able to move your stop loss just below that
one. So now as the market rallies up and starts to dip, drip lower in here, we have not made any new
higher highs. Market eventually comes down and boom, takes you right out, right? That's your stop. And
what happens as a result? Are you upset about it? No. You controlled your risk. Did you make money in this
example? Hypothetically, you would have.

So that's one example using the stop-loss rules and applying the weekly ranges and such. Let's look at
again, assuming we got in at 1.3515 and you got stopped out on your whatever the balance would be,
that's 66 pips. Do you remember at the beginning of this video series we talked about how 30 to 50 pips a
week you can build a career on? There's 60 pips later at your feet. Did you trade every single day? No. Did
you beat your head against the wall trying to figure out where the next bat pattern is, where the next crab
pattern is, the next ICT reflection is? No.
You You would have a clear-cut routine that you would look for specifically every single week. You should
know what you're going to be looking for. Are you going to be looking to be a buyer or a seller or sitting on
your hands? Or are you preserving your capital because you're already in position? You're either
managing, executing, or sitting on your hands. Simple.

Now with all that, the topic of daily ranges will be much, much more involved in the eighth installment of this
series because we're going to talk about the five-day average daily range. We're just going to briefly
mention it here, but I'm going to reserve a much more in-depth discussion until the eighth video because
I'm actually going to have the tool released at the time I do the video upload as well. So, for those of you
who don't have the 5-day average daily range indicator for MT4, you'll be able to get it for free. I'll share it
with you. That's the one you see me using in my videos.

Regardless of what type of trader you are, we're going to be talking about trade classifications now. Why is
it important and what's the big deal? Number one, if you are short-term trading, for instance, everything
was bullish going into this week here, maybe you saw this as a selling opportunity on the lower timeframe if
you, this is an optimal trade entry that you, you sold into. And the market came down, and you were, you
thinking, wow, I'm really smart, I'm making money. But all of a sudden, you married a vein; the higher
timeframe intermediate term trade setup based on a daily timeframe suggests that this thing's going higher.
Technically can it always unfold like that? No. Sometimes you're going to have where the technicals
suggest that it may continue to go higher but it doesn't do it, and that's when you get to climax reversals.
We're not covering that in this series. It's important that you understand that that's not what I'm trying to
teach you here. I'm trying to teach you to trade within that big lion's portion of the move, that's all you need.
You don't need to be specific highs and lows, you don't need that. So that's the powerful concepts coming
through and bleeding out all the high-end nervousness that is presented with traders to feel like they get
the highs and lows. You don't need that. Notice that this trade setup was not below the week. The low of the
week was for Monday. This was a nice move. I'm not going to argue with anybody that would be short this.
Say you shorted up in this area here around the 80, come down here around, you know, that's 60 pips.
There's nothing wrong with that, that's equivalent to what we would have made on this trade. So what?
That's not your trade. That's not your trade, and that's how you look at it. Guys can come on the forums all
day long, I took this trade and I took that. Who cares? You can't make any money on that trade, and you
can't lose any money on it either. You can't gain any glory and you can't swallow any pride over either, so
what difference does it make? While it's educational and inspiring to see other people's results, do not
assume that that should have been traded. You should have sucked, why did you do the homework on that
trade going into it? Because if you didn't do it, it's not your trade. So keep that in mind. You have no reason,
no reason whatsoever to worry about what the next guy is doing, none.

So that 5-day average daily range is going to be crucial to us in the eighth video, but for now we're just
going to understand that we're going to have to learn to take something off regardless of what type of
trader we're trading with, in either intermediate term, short term, day trade or scalping. But if you are
marrying the vein here and looking to be a seller on this Tuesday with an overall higher level intermediate
term bullishness underway, you will marry this vein here and you'll, if you don't manage your stops right,
you can get killed. Look at it, it didn't take very long for this market to come back up to that high here. So all
those folks that were shorting here and didn't use proper trade management and risk controls and
preserving capital and taking profits as the market moves lower, if you were thinking get in, I'm going to get
out all my profit at my target, that's it, you see they're all or nothing, well, guess what? You know, you got
nothing. That's what this video is going to be dealing with, why it's important for you to take something off
because you're never going to be 100 percent. As long as I've been doing this, almost 20 years, I'm not
always right. I'm not always right to where it's going to go to. So I work within a 75 to 80 percent range of
what I think the move's going to do. I drop back down to about 80 percent, sometimes even 70 percent of
what I expect to see in terms of magnitude moving higher or lower, and that's what I'm trying to get to. I'll let
the rest of these guys try to beat their chests and say look, man, I got all the move, good, I applaud you,
but over time you will not be doing that. I guarantee you, you will not be doing that. So don't go into this
game thinking that's what you should be doing.

section_title: TRADING IN SYNC VS. OUT OF SYNC
content:
Now with the 5-day average daily range mentioned briefly that you should be always trying to take some
profit off there, let's talk about multiple timeframe trading. Multiple timeframe trading, again, we discussed
obviously the daily chart is used for any term trading or swing trading. Then you have the short term
trading, which is classified, and the analysis is done with a 4-hour chart. Then you have the day trades that
are arrived by analysis on a 1-hour chart, and then anything at 15 minutes or 5 minutes is obviously
scalping. So why say scalping? I'm not talking three pips and five pips and six pips, that's not, to me that's
it's goober trading. If you can't at least make 15 to 20 pips on the setup, don't even do it. Now, how am I
saying if you can't make, there's no guarantee that anything's going to unfold at such like reward or risk,
there's no guarantee that those things are painting out, but if you don't at least plan to hold a trade to make
at least 15 to 20 pips if it moves favorable for you, then it should not be taken as a trade. That's just the way
I look at it. You may argue with you and say, you know, I, well, I can make 10 pips 20 times a day, great,
then you're a good trader and I'll leave it there. But you're watching this video wanting to know what I do,
not necessarily having to do it, but I look at the market where if I can't make at least 15 and 20 pips or at
least willing to hold it that and that magnitude of pips, I'm not taking the trade. And that's usually where I'm
using you trying to either take something off the trade or aggressively move to stop up to even or taking
some of the risk off at least.
So now because we classify the trades in terms of intermediate term, short term, day trades and scalping,
we have to also understand that there is a classification for trading in sync and out of sync. And what do I
mean by that? Well, the two higher time frames, which is the daily and 4-hour, if you're trading in those
timeframe directional premise, then you are classified as trading in sync regardless of what timeframe
you're trading. Now, you can trade counter-trend or counter-sync if you are out of sync rather as a day
trader and a scalper, where you can actually short-term trade, you know, counter the higher timeframe. But
you got to be very, very nimble doing that, so I'm not going to advocate doing that in this video series, but
you see me a lot of times in my market reviews or my trade recaps that I do on YouTube, you know, it may
go against what the higher time frames are saying, but I'm still netting pips. That comes with experience. I
do it just to show you that you can make money without a directional premise or bias, but it's just better for
your trades if you're trading in sync with that daily and/or 4-hour time frames. So if you're day trading in
sync, that means you're going to, if the higher timeframe daily or 4-hour is bullish, then your day trades, if
you're a buyer, then you're trading in sync. Just remember that if you're selling in a day trade or scalping
short while the daily and or a 4-hour are bullish, you are out of sync, or your counter-trend trading. That's a
very, very important rule to understand in terms of your analysis.

section_title: THE NECESSITY OF TAKING PARTIAL PROFITS
content:
Because if you can't generically define your market environments like that, or your trading within the current
environment, in my opinion, you're trading in the dark. You will not be rule-based. You'll be seeing moves
like this in here, it's really dropping off really handsomely, and you're going to think, well, it's going to
probably come down here deeper. Well, it may not, as you see here it doesn't, and it starts to rally the other
way. That's very demoralizing if you haven't taken some profits off as the market moves lower, and if you
didn't even adjust your stop, the guys that want to sell up here and I'm going to keep my stop here and take
all or nothing, well, you got nothing again for the time and effort you put into the market. It's important that
you have something out, take something off the trade.
It doesn't make a difference if it was 20 bucks, you got 20 bucks, it's 20 more dollars than you had before
you put the trade on. I used to be, I'm going to get my exit or I'm going to get stopped out and that's just the
way it is, and man, I got chewed up. You just can't do that as a developing trader because number one,
your mind's not going to let you do it. You're going to start saying, well, you know, it took this many losses,
so I'm going to be overtrading, I'm going to take more trades now, and I'm going to widen my stop because
I know I'm right, or I'm going to be buying more and over-leveraging, and I'm going to get out sooner than I
should because I just need to get that money back. And it just causes all kinds of havoc, you know,
emotionally and psychologically, and then you forget about what you're supposed to be doing because
you're focusing too much on the pain. So by having these rules in place, it helps you define what it is
specifically you should be doing, and that alone is what your focus should be. Cause think about it, if your
perspective is limited to just those things, do you have time to be worrying about where it's going to go to?
If you already have a stop loss defined, you're either going to watch your 6 and collapse 50 percent on the
trade or you're going to go let it go down and hit your stop, period, that's it. What's the worry about? If
you're worrying, it's because you probably put too much on the trade or you've probably been trading too
much. It's just that simple, guys, but we want to overanalyze it and blame something else outside of us. I
get a million emails asking what it is that makes me do the wrong things. Well, and apparently that's human
nature, and we've said this earlier, if you see a sign that warns and says, don't walk on the grass, what do
you barely want to do as a kid? If you have children, my kids want to do it all the time. Don't touch wet
paint, man, they got paint all over their hands. Oshkosh because it's got paint all over it. Their new Nikes,
hmm, it's got that new stain that was on the picnic table that we were going through the park and says,
don't touch this, it's got wet paint on it, got a little rope around it. Well, they're going to go through the rope,
cross the red tape if you will, do the hazard warning and I'm going to do the very thing that you're not
supposed to do. That's human nature, so that gets back to, are you going to be a rule-based individual?
Because if you can't follow rules, you're not going to do well with this business. You have to be, you have to
be disciplined and have it defined very, very clearly what it is specifically that you should be doing. If your
trade plan is very detailed, one can argue I have too many moving parts in my, in my plan. I don't have a
whole lot of moving parts in my trading plan. I have a whole lot of moving parts in my understanding how
the markets themselves work, that's why I'm so effective, that's why my analysis before it happens is so
accurate. That's the premise to why I'm doing these things because I want to communicate that to you. You
can do these things, but it's going to take time for you to overcome the 'you'. So by having a classification of
your trades and understand what you're trading and why you're trading, it will help you define where you're
going to take profits at. So avoid that I'm getting all of my maximum profit, don't do that. Because first of all,
you're not going to be right more times than you think you are, and you just want to pay yourself something.
And having that mindset that I'm going to have the maximum profit always, because think about when you
first started, you got in there, you looked at the charts, what are you calculating? Said me, I'm going to
make 20 pips a day, I can do this, this, this, this, and all of a sudden in nine months you're a millionaire one
five an hour training camp. It doesn't work like that, guys, it does not work like that. You have to overcome a
whole lot of internal things before these charts ever do anything for you. So the way you grind that out is
begin with a rule-based system, and the more rules you have that are clearly defined, the less room there
is for emotional, fear-based and greed-based trading. It's just that simple.

section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT SPLIT GAIN RATIOS
content:
So now let's move on and take a look at the ICT split gain ratios. We're going to be looking at the
intermediate term ratios. Now, what is a split gain ratio? Well, it's something that is really classified just for
teaching purposes because I don't have any real specific 'I do this every single time', I don't have that. So
I'm giving you these ideas to stimulate your decision-making in your own trading. So don't think for a minute
that this is always going to unfold exactly like this. It just obviously means for you, you need to stimulate
your decision-making, and you can come up with your own way of using these as examples to arrive at
what you're most comfortable doing. And the only way you're going to learn which one you're comfortable
doing is by just simply jumping in the pool and doing it.

So an intermediate term ratio would be how am I going to take my profits, assuming that you have a
100,000 dollar leverage in the trade. In other words, you have what we considered a standard lot in the
Euro. That would be 100,000 dollars, but each pip is going to be worth 10 dollars per movement up or
down, gain or loss out of your account. In this example, for intermediate term ratios, if you are in sync
trading in the higher timeframe daily and/or 4-hour directional premise, you can take your profits in stages
of 20, 20, 20, 20, 20. In other words, out of 100 percent of that total position, you could take 20,000 of
leverage off at your first target, at your second target take another 20,000 of leverage off, take another
20,000 off at the third target, fourth, and final fifth. That's like an ideal scenario if you want to scale off in
five position exit points. Five is a whole lot, but again, we're talking intermediate term trading.
So you could take that first 20 percent off at the average daily range for that particular day you took the
trade on. And then the next 20 percent could come off at a previous daily high or an intra-week high, and
then the next one could be a monthly high, or we could start looking at Fibonacci 127 and 162 extensions.
Any one of those things could be applied here. But it's a matter of how much am I taking off. If you're going
to take out for scaling, you could do 25, 25, 25, and 25. But where you're taking those profits at? You're
taking 2,500 of the 100,000 that you put the trade on at, at logical levels of profit taking, old highs, old lows,
Fibonacci extensions, previous range retracements. In other words, like a move say this is high here down
to this low. As price moves up into what would be considered the 79 percent retracement level here, if you
were long back in here, you would be looking to take some profits in here. And then obviously, if it gets to
this level here, we could take profits there as well, and then price starts to retrace. Assuming that we were
able to, you know, be a part of the next leg up, if you were buying in here, this old high would be an
example, this old high would be an example, and then the extensions beyond that.

- CONTINUED
Like this, if we have our fib pulled down to that low, our 127 would be an ideal level to take profits at, the
162 would be a nice level, and ultimately our higher level profit objective is a 200 extension right here. So
it's using all the concepts together in a framework where it allows you to just really plan your exits and look
for reasons to do it, not simply because I made this much money, where are we reaching for in terms of
price action and applying all of our tools. If you want to scale out in three stages, you could do 20 percent
of the position and then 40 and 40, holding on to those last two portions at 40 and 40 because you want to
be hopefully holding a larger percentage of the trade because you're trading in a higher timeframe
directional premise, or would be considered in sync.

If you're trading out of sync and you're against the daily and or 4-hour, you must scale in five stages. You
can take 40 percent off initially at a price objective and then 10, 10, 10, and 10, leaving small portions on.
And even if just for sake of luck, even though you're against the higher time frames, you could still manage
to get some pretty good gains like we have here and like we have here. So it was against the higher time
frames, but you're still able to, you know, hold a little bit a piece of the position and maybe catch some
more of a, you know, volatility in your trade.
If you want to scale out of four stages, you could do 50 percent of the position and 30, so you got 80
percent of the trade off. Then you can hold on for the final 20 percent, scaling off in 10 and 10. Then you
have three stage scaling; if you want to take 60 percent off, that would leave 40 percent. You can scale off
in two more stages at 20 percent. Again, using logical levels of price levels and Fibonacci, simply applying
that the overlapping of reasons or confluences for, for price to be moving to those levels. That's where you
would be doing these, these split gain ratios. In other words, where I'm going to be taking my position and
how much of the position that I initially put on would I be taking off.

Now this gets back to your trade entry. You can do this several different ways. You can do it, you can set
your position up and do it as individual orders. In other words, if you're trading 100,000, you can for the
assuming that you're trading in sync and you want to scale out at three, three levels, you could have your
entry limit order, three separate entry limit orders to get the same price. Then your first of the three, you
could look to take profit off at a first objective. And but the positions would be set up in such a way that you
would have 20,000 leverage, 40,000 leverage, and then 40,000 leverage. So you're going to take the first
20,000 off at your first price objectives. Remaining two positions would be open, and then the second one
would come off at another logical level of price objective. And then once it's triggered, the remaining order
of 40,000 dollars would be setting in the marketplace and either it gets to its position or stopped out or
whatever.
Or you could just do the trade as 100,000 dollars and then go in and set your limit order to take partials off.
Some platforms will allow you to do that. I don't know what every platform each of you are using, you just
got to look and see what is available to you.

Let's move on to the next one. Short-term ratios, again assuming that we're trading in sync with the daily,
the in sync ratios would be taking 30 percent off if you're scaling out at three. In this case, it looks a little
different because I'm taking the smaller one first because I want to have the maximum amount of leverage
still in the marketplace in my position. So I'm going to take 30 percent off, and then I'll take 35 percent off
the second profit objective, and then remaining 35 percent would be taken at the third and ultimate price
objective that I would have for that short-term trade. 25 percent if I want to scale out in two stages, 25
percent at the first one, and then 75 percent at the second price objective.

- EXAMPLE
To give you an illustration of what that might look like is if I'm trading with a 4-hour chart, and it all I did was
just gear down, I didn't do anything, I didn't pick anything, it just gave me this chart here. Assume for a
moment that I went short here on this optimal trade entry right in here and back into this order block in
here. So if I went short in here around that 1.3080 level, the first price objective would be down here, this
old low which is the reaction low from this point here down here. That would be my first price objective.
Again, scaling out for a short-term trade at that point here, it would be a 146 pip position, I'm sorry, 146
pips for the first scaling out.
So if I'm scaling out in two stages, 25 percent would come off the trade right there at 140 some pips. The
next point of the ratio would be 75 percent, I would take that out at either the 127 extension or the 162
extension conservatively. But it's going to say the 127, we're not going to say we got everything down to
here. If you got to the 127, the remaining portion that would be in the trade would profit hypothetically
obviously to that price point here of 191 pips. And just for sake of completeness, if you use the 162
extension as your final position, you'd make 248, and if you held for the 200 extension, you can make 310
pips, and you would just do the math on what you wouldn't make or risk on that trade.

If you're trading out of sync, and for short-term trading, you want to take larger portions off sooner. So you
would take 60 percent of the trade off and then two remaining positions at 20 and 20. And again, all you're
doing is looking for levels to take your profits out at that ratio in terms of your open position. Initially, you're
scaling off a percentage. In other words, in this case, out of sync, you would take 60 percent off initially at
your first target, and then 20 percent off at your second target, and then 20 percent remaining would come
off at your third and ultimate price level.
And a good example that would be 60 percent off at an old high or low that you used your fib from, and
then 127 would be coming off at 20 percent, and then 162 extension or 200 percent would come off on that
final 20 percent of the initial position. And if you want to do two stages on scaling that you're out of sync
with on the higher time frames, you would take 80 percent off initially and then let your 20 percent
remaining in the position go to your second and final objective. And obviously, you can do double tap, which
is 50-50, where you take your first objective is half the position comes off, move to break even, and then
you hold for your second objective, and you don't care what happens at that point.

Moving on to our day trading ratios. Looking at this, if you're trading in sync, you take 30 percent off of your
position on the first target, and on the second target, take 60 percent off, and then you would leave 10
percent to see what happens. Because again, just because you're day trading doesn't mean you can't try to
capitalize on a move that would most likely continue on for several days. Again, day trading, don't think I've
got to get out all of my position today. Take a good chunk of the market out and move to no risk and lock in
profits, but leave a little piece on there because you may catch a lot more of the daily range increase going
into Friday, or it may even continue going for, you know, weeks if it works its way where such your stop is
never tagged, you'd still be in the market as it continues to move in your favor.
Obviously, if you move out in three stages, and you have all three timeframes in sync, you could take 20
percent off, 20 percent off at your first two targets, and then leave 60 percent remaining for your ultimate
objective. That's again, only if you're trading with all timeframes in sync and everything is loaded for it to
move up. And then if you do it in two stages, you would just take 20 percent off at your first target and leave
80 percent for your ultimate price objective. In other words, it lowers, if you were using a day trading entry
to get in sync with a position, or an intermediate term trade based on the daily timeframe. So in other
words, that's the reason why you would be holding 80 percent, just taking 20 percent off and holding 80 for,
you know, as much as weeks.

And if you're trading out of sync, you would take obviously in three stages, you would take 80 percent of the
position off at your first target. Two remaining positions you can take off at 10 percent and 10 percent,
giving you your full 100 percent off. And then two scaling stages you could do against the higher timeframe,
you would take 70 percent of the position trade off, remaining 25 percent you would take at your second
price objective. And obviously, if you want to, you simply just do double tap when you're unclear. In other
words, if you just don't know if you're in sync or not and you're, your day trading, it's just simply, it's easy,
you just do double tap. Take half the position off at your first objective, go to break even, and then look for
your second objective, 127 or 162 extension, period, short and sweet, nothing fancy.

Moving on to our last ratio, this is scalping ratios. Again, this is for 15 to 30 pips range, that type of trading.
Either hold for your stop to be hit or your target, and you take a loss or you decrease risk initially on the
trade or you go to break-even. In other words, if you're able to trim some of that risk away, that's pretty
much, and that's what you're trying to do. In an ideal world, if everything's in sync, that's how you trade it.
You look for your stop-loss at maximum loss or target, or if it allows you to trim some of your risk away, you
move your stop-loss closer to break-even, eventually into profit, but that's, that's, that's scalping for you.
I'm not going to try to give you too many things for scalping because I really don't think traders should be
doing that, but you know, there's a people group out there that wants to hear about it so there you go. Or
you can simply just use double tap, as soon as you get to 15 to 30 pips, you take, you know, 50 percent of
the position off, and you can leave position open and catch anything that would be in sync with the higher
time frames. If you're counter-trend or out of sync from the 4-hour and daily, always watch your 6. You
would look to take 50 percent of the trade off if the trade is questionable and just manage it like you would
if you were trading in sync.

section_title: FLEXIBILITY AND PLANNING FOR PROFIT EXITS
content:
So it's really a generic way of looking at how I scale out profits and how I manage the position. I generally
have what ratio in mind before I even put the trade on. The only time that it will deviate is when I'm in an
intermediate term trade. I may elect to take more or less of the position off at the second, third, fourth, and
fifth stages. It may become a three-stage trade where I take all of my profits if it moves in my favor versus a
five-stage scaling out. In other words, if I think it was going to move to the 200 percent extension and I'm in
that move favorably and I've taken off my first two scaling stages, that third, fourth, and fifth actually may
just drop down to I'm just going to take it all at the fourth, or I'm going to drop it down to what would be
considered the opening remaining position of the trade, say it's 30 percent of the trade. I may take, you
know, 15 percent off at one level and then 15 percent off at another level.
So again, don't think that you're locked into these ratios. This is a means of you deciding what you want to
do at price levels that we've talked about in this course, that being old highs and lows that you use your
reference points for your fibs, your 127 extension, 162 extension, and then your 200 extension. That's it,
there's nothing more to it, there's nothing, you know, difficult about it. It's just a matter of planning your
trade around that analysis and where those levels are. How much you're going to do of profit taking when it
gets there. And preferably, you want to have those in form of limit orders. You can always manage the limit
orders after they're already in place. Treat them like stop-loss orders because many times you'll be
surprised how fast some, some markets will move and you'll get to your first objective, if you didn't get that,
and it recedes back to your stop-loss again, very demoralizing. So I counsel you to, you know, to use these
as templates if you will, to frame your, your trading and your, your level of scaling out profits. That's really all
there is to it, there's no magic behind it. What you see here for, you know, 20-80 could very easily be, you
know, 70 and 30, you know, there's no iron set of rules where you have to do it this way and only. It's, it's a
unique thing for us as traders, we can tailor this stuff to our own personality. So again, it's just a means of
stimulating decision-making on your part. Scale out something and really to stimulate and try to, you know,
inject the idea that you should be taking something as profits as the trade unfolds because just simply
because you think it's going to go up to a specific price level, there's no guarantee it's doing that, and it
may not get there without coming back down to another previous level and it may be the level your stop is
set. So pay yourself, get something out of the trade. You know, you, you've invested time and effort and
probably fear and worry about missing the move in the trade setup, so once you're in there, you get some
profits, get some of it out.

So that's pretty much it for this teaching session in this, in this video. I just want to give you some ideas
about what we're going to be doing in the eighth episode and how to get prepared for it. I would counsel
you obviously go back through all the previous videos and that includes this one. Go through your notes,
make sure you have a good collection of notes, and read those notes. You don't have to see the videos
over and again once you go through it one more time. But your notes, you should read them at least once a
day, whether you trade or not, read them once a day because by doing that, it'll constantly feed your mind
and you remember these things, and you see certain things unfolding your charts, oh yeah, that's that thing
he was talking about or that's that event that usually takes place. And then you'll start to learn how to
engage the marketplace, and price action will become a thing of expectancy. You'll expect these things, not
what's going to happen. When I sit down at the charts, I'm not asking what's going to happen, I'm expecting
something to happen. There's a difference. If it, if that went over your head, rewind again, listen to that. It's
a very different perspective. As a professional trader, I anticipate something, whereas novice traders are
expecting something. What's going to happen? You know, you're asking everybody on the phone, what do
you think the Euro's going to do? What difference does what I say make? Because really, are you going to
trade on what I'm telling you? You shouldn't. So have that in your mind as you go through your videos
again, get some good notes, and we're going to be moving into a really, really cool final episode, and a lot
of guys that have been on the fence about my concepts will, I think, will be pleasantly surprised on how
useful that video will be. Until then, guys, I wish you good luck and good trading.

Hope you enjoyed this installment and coming up in the last and eighth in our series is 'Escape-and
- Evasion'. Now, this is pretty much like my be-all-end-all for application for my tools. I get a lot of flack from
the guys that aren't into me or into the tools or I just want to be, you know, adversarial, they'll say that I
have too many components. And while I have a very vast understanding of market analysis concepts and
techniques, I'm not always using every single tool. So it's like a carpenter, you know, they may have a
toolbox full of all kinds of tools, but you're not really driving a screw in with a ruler. So, you know, there's
certain times that you need a specific tool or where a tool is multifunctional. But I did my very, very best to
try to concise, condense rather, and make it more concise as to when I reach for a specific tool or
application or a technique or concept and when I avoid not using it at all. And I set it up in such a different
way, it's like a flow chart. And I think what you'll be surprised is that I've applied how I sit down and I look at
the charts from a top-down analysis down to the smaller timeframes and what goes through my mind as I
do the analysis. And it's kind of like an if-then format.

section_title: THE ICT FLOW CHART FOR MARKET ANALYSIS AND TOOL APPLICATIONS
content:
So if the market is presenting me this specific criteria, then I will do this, this or that. And if those functions
are met with, you know, even more in-market conditions, then I move to another stage in my analysis. So it
allows you to remove all the ambiguity, all of the fog, if you will. It's a, I have a lot of military themed
concepts or titles or, you know, teaching templates, and that's just my personality. I, you know, I've never
been in the military, but I think by having this framework, it'll allow you to have like a plan of attack, and it
will give you a means of, you know, systematically attacking the market and then you doing your own, you
know, research and recon, if you will, and then you're moving out into the fields and doing that your very
own without having any kind of input or stimulus on my part or anyone else's, because that's really what
we're getting at. We're trying to be building a measure of consistency, obviously a means of limiting risk as
best we can. We can't remove it 100 percent, but we're going to do our very best in our trading to try to do
that very thing. And at the end of the day, we should be hopefully moving towards profitability. It's never
guaranteed. I can't guarantee it. No other mentor, teacher, or author of courses can do that.

section_title: RISK MANAGEMENT AND TRADER DEVELOPMENT IN PART 8
content:
So while it is my goal to try to give you a roadmap, if you will, on how I break down and digest the
marketplace, it will still, and I'm promising you, it will still incur losses if you trade with real money with
these concepts, any of these concepts, not just simply what's revealed in the eighth and final video, but any
of these concepts, you are still inherently taking on risk that will invariably result in you losing money. So
you have to be very responsible and trade with a demo account only. When you think that you're ready to
do it with live money, you take that decision and you make it your own and only responsibility comes along
with it. If you have success with these concepts, obviously I'm, I'm more than excited to hear what each of
you are doing as you're developing, and that's the, that's my motivation in doing all these things. I don't take
any monetary gain out of it. I don't request any kind of subscription fees or anything like that. I get a high off
of everyone else's development, and when I see the feedback through either Twitter or emails or on forums
that they've, you know, developed to a measure of, you know, consistently profitable or encouraging results
that lead to more, you know, comfort levels in terms of not being fearful of taking a trade. You'd be
surprised how many folks are still fearful, you know, just getting involved in this in a demo account because
they don't want to face that, that right or wrong conclusion to their decision.

section_title: OVERVIEW OF "ESCAPE-AND-EVASION" CONTENT AND FINAL THOUGHTS
content:
I'll have more to say about that in the last installment, but what specifically will be covered in the 'Escape
- and-Evasion'? Well, we're going to be overviewing the entire ICT Scout Sniper Basic Field Guide series in
its completeness because we have to do this in a more comprehensive fashion. It just means we've got to
go through it all over again, but obviously, it's not going to go 12 hours long, but we're going to break it
down and be more precise and concise with what specifically you're supposed to be doing with each one of
these videos and the tools that are within them. And we're, we're talking about adopting the ICT mindset to
trading.
We're looking at the Inner Circle Trader tips for continued trader development. A lot of things that I have not
spoken in this series, I've held on to for the last video so that it's at least six hours long. I'm just kidding, it's
going to be long, but it's probably not going to be six hours. And we're going to be revealing the ICT flow
chart to market analysis and tool applications so that way you don't have to guess or wonder, would I be
using this tool at this time or would I be using that method at this time? No, you're going to have a very
clear, generic way of going through the market, breaking it down in a routine fashion. That way, once you
do it for a period of time, you won't need the flow chart, you'll just know simply, okay, well it does this, so
therefore I'm going to do that. If it's done this, then I'm going to do that. So you're always going to have a
response to the current environment. So either you're going to take action, you're going to sit on your
hands, or you're going to protect and preserve your capital. There's your three engagements to the
marketplace, that's it, it's all you can do. Either you're going to take action, you're going to sit on your hands
and do nothing, or you're going to protect and preserve your equity. There's only three things that can
happen in the marketplace as you as a trader, and you'll have every possible scenario that I could fathom in
a manner of looking at the marketplace, digesting it, and saying, okay, what should I be doing in the
mindset that ICT or myself utilizes in trading? And obviously, like all good things, they have to come to an
end. I'll have a few more words on accountability and consistency. So until then, guys, I wish you good luck
and good trading.
